instruction: What is the difference between a dense layer and a sparse layer?
output: A dense layer connects every input neuron to every output neuron — all weight matrix entries are non-zero and contribute to computation. A sparse layer has most connections missing or zero — only a subset of inputs influence each output. Think of it like a fully connected office where everyone emails everyone versus a sparse one with defined communication paths. Dense layers are computationally expensive but expressive; sparse layers are cheaper but require careful design to preserve representational power. Mixture of Experts is a form of structured sparsity — only certain experts are activated per input. A common misconception is that sparse layers are always less capable — sparse networks found by lottery ticket hypothesis match dense network performance with far fewer connections. In one line: dense layers connect all neurons with full weight matrices; sparse layers use mostly zero connections — structured sparsity enables efficiency while preserving expressiveness when connection patterns are well-designed.

instruction: What is max pooling vs average pooling?
output: Max pooling takes the maximum value within a pooling window — selecting the most strongly activated feature in each region. Average pooling computes the mean of all values in the window — smoothing the representation. Think of max pooling as a highlight reel that captures the peak moment of each play, versus average pooling as the average statistics for an entire season. Max pooling captures the presence of a feature anywhere in the region; average pooling captures the overall intensity across the region. For image classification, max pooling dominates because detecting feature presence matters more than average intensity. A common misconception is that max pooling is always better — global average pooling (averaging the entire feature map) is used effectively in many modern architectures for final classification layers. In one line: max pooling captures the strongest feature activation in each region; average pooling captures the mean — max pooling detects feature presence, average pooling captures overall intensity, with max pooling preferred for feature detection tasks.

instruction: What is a receptive field in CNNs?
output: The receptive field is the region of the input that influences a particular neuron's activation — deeper neurons have larger receptive fields because they are influenced by larger input regions through stacked convolutions. Think of it like field of vision — a neuron in the first layer sees only a small patch of the image, while a neuron in the fifth layer sees most of the image through the chain of convolutions. Increasing receptive field enables detection of larger patterns. Dilated convolutions expand the receptive field without increasing parameters by skipping positions in the convolution kernel. A common misconception is that the theoretical receptive field describes what the network actually uses — in practice, effective receptive fields (the input region that most influences a neuron) are significantly smaller than theoretical maximum. In one line: the receptive field is the input region influencing a neuron — deeper neurons see larger regions through stacked convolutions, enabling detection of progressively larger patterns at higher network layers.

instruction: What is the difference between a kernel and a filter in CNNs?
output: A kernel is a single 2D matrix of weights applied at one spatial location during convolution. A filter is the collection of kernels applied across all input channels — a filter for a color image has three kernels (one per RGB channel), each applied to the corresponding input channel, with results summed. Think of a filter like a multi-lens camera attachment where each lens (kernel) captures one color channel and the results combine into a single feature map. This terminology is often used interchangeably, causing confusion. The output of applying one filter to an input is a single feature map; with n filters you get n feature maps. A common misconception is that kernel and filter are always synonymous — kernel strictly refers to the per-channel weight matrix while filter refers to the complete multi-channel parameter set. In one line: a kernel is a single-channel convolution weight matrix; a filter is the collection of kernels applied across all input channels — one filter produces one feature map, and n filters produce n feature maps.

instruction: What is depth-wise separable convolution?
output: Depth-wise separable convolution factors a standard convolution into two steps: a depth-wise convolution (applying one kernel per input channel independently) followed by a 1×1 pointwise convolution (mixing channels). Think of it like processing each audio track separately (depth-wise) then mixing them (pointwise) — you achieve the same final result as processing everything together but with far fewer operations. MobileNet and Xception use depth-wise separable convolutions to reduce computation by 8-9x versus standard convolutions. A common misconception is that depth-wise separable convolutions always match standard convolution quality — they slightly reduce expressiveness by decoupling spatial and channel mixing, with minimal quality loss in most practical applications. In one line: depth-wise separable convolutions factor standard convolutions into per-channel spatial filtering plus channel mixing, reducing computation by 8-9x with minimal quality loss — the core efficiency technique in MobileNet architectures.

instruction: What is the inception module?
output: The inception module applies multiple convolution filter sizes (1×1, 3×3, 5×5) and max pooling in parallel to the same input, concatenating their outputs — allowing the network to capture patterns at multiple scales simultaneously. Think of it like examining a painting with multiple magnifications simultaneously and combining all perspectives — fine details (small kernels), medium patterns (medium kernels), and coarse structure (large kernels or pooling) all contribute to the representation. 1×1 convolutions reduce channel dimensions before larger convolutions, making inception computationally feasible. A common misconception is that inception modules simply apply more filters — the key innovation is multi-scale parallel processing and the computational efficiency trick of 1×1 bottlenecks. In one line: inception modules capture multi-scale patterns by applying multiple filter sizes in parallel and concatenating results, using 1×1 bottlenecks for computational efficiency.

instruction: What is the difference between valid and same padding in CNNs?
output: Valid padding applies no padding — the convolution only operates where the kernel fully overlaps with the input, reducing spatial dimensions. Same padding adds zeros around the input border to ensure output spatial dimensions match input dimensions. Think of valid padding like a stamp that can only be placed fully within the canvas, versus same padding like extending the canvas with blank space so the stamp can be placed anywhere. Valid padding is computationally cheaper but progressively shrinks spatial dimensions; same padding preserves dimensions throughout the network. A common misconception is that same padding is always preferable — valid padding is used in architectures where explicit spatial reduction per layer is desired without pooling. In one line: valid padding produces smaller outputs by only convolving where the kernel fully fits; same padding preserves spatial dimensions by zero-padding the input — same padding is standard when spatial dimension preservation is desired.

instruction: What is the difference between upsampling and transposed convolution?
output: Upsampling (nearest neighbor or bilinear interpolation) increases spatial dimensions using fixed mathematical rules — no learned parameters. Transposed convolution (sometimes called deconvolution) learns upsampling parameters — it is a learnable inverse of convolution that expands spatial dimensions. Think of upsampling like stretching a photograph using a fixed interpolation algorithm versus transposed convolution like learning the best way to zoom in for a specific task. Transposed convolutions are used in generators (GANs), decoders (VAEs), and segmentation networks (U-Net) where learned upsampling improves quality. A common misconception is that transposed convolution reverses the forward convolution — it has the same connectivity pattern but learned independently, not the mathematical inverse. In one line: upsampling uses fixed interpolation to increase spatial dimensions; transposed convolutions learn the upsampling weights — transposed convolutions are preferred when task-specific spatial expansion improves output quality.

instruction: What is semantic segmentation?
output: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in an image — classifying each pixel as belonging to a semantic category (road, sky, person, car) without distinguishing between instances of the same class. Think of it like coloring in a coloring book where every pixel gets a color corresponding to its category — all people are one color, all cars another. Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN) and U-Net are foundational architectures. A common misconception is that semantic and instance segmentation are the same — semantic segmentation treats all cars as one class; instance segmentation distinguishes individual car instances. In one line: semantic segmentation classifies every pixel into a category label, producing dense class maps for the entire image without distinguishing between individual instances of the same class.

instruction: What is instance segmentation?
output: Instance segmentation combines object detection and semantic segmentation — identifying individual object instances and producing pixel-level masks for each one, distinguishing between separate objects of the same class. Think of it like the difference between painting all people red (semantic) and painting each individual person a different shade of red (instance) — instance segmentation knows that there are three distinct people, not just regions of 'person'. Mask R-CNN extends Faster R-CNN with a parallel mask prediction head. A common misconception is that instance segmentation is simply semantic segmentation with counting — it requires simultaneous detection, classification, and pixel-level localization, which are fundamentally different tasks. In one line: instance segmentation provides pixel-level masks for each individual object instance, combining object detection and semantic segmentation to distinguish between separate instances of the same class.

instruction: What is object detection?
output: Object detection identifies the presence, location (bounding boxes), and class of all objects in an image — answering both 'what is in this image?' and 'where is it?' simultaneously. Think of it like annotating a photograph with labels and rectangles around every object — the detector outputs a list of (class, bounding box, confidence) tuples. Two-stage detectors (Faster R-CNN) first propose regions then classify them; one-stage detectors (YOLO, SSD) predict boxes and classes simultaneously. A common misconception is that classification and detection are similar tasks — detection requires simultaneous localization of potentially many objects, which is fundamentally more complex than classifying a single centered object. In one line: object detection identifies all objects in an image with their class labels and bounding box locations, with architectures ranging from two-stage (accurate but slow) to one-stage (fast but slightly less accurate).

instruction: What is non-maximum suppression (NMS)?
output: Non-maximum suppression filters duplicate detection boxes — after an object detector produces many overlapping bounding boxes for the same object, NMS selects the highest-confidence box and suppresses all others with high overlap (above an IoU threshold). Think of it like choosing the best photo from a burst of identical shots — you keep the sharpest one and discard the near-duplicates. Without NMS, detectors would output dozens of boxes for each object. IoU (Intersection over Union) measures box overlap to determine which boxes are duplicates. A common misconception is that NMS is a learned component — it is a fixed post-processing rule, though differentiable NMS variants exist. In one line: NMS post-processes detector outputs by suppressing all but the highest-confidence bounding box among heavily overlapping candidates, eliminating duplicate detections of the same object.

instruction: What is intersection over union (IoU)?
output: IoU measures the overlap between two bounding boxes — dividing the area of their intersection by the area of their union, producing a value between 0 (no overlap) and 1 (perfect overlap). Think of it like measuring how well two rectangles match — if they perfectly coincide IoU=1, if they barely touch IoU approaches 0. IoU is used in object detection to determine whether a predicted box matches a ground truth box (typically IoU > 0.5 is a correct detection), in NMS to filter duplicate boxes, and as a loss function component. A common misconception is that IoU > 0.5 is the universally correct threshold — different applications use different thresholds (COCO uses 0.5-0.95) depending on required localization precision. In one line: IoU measures bounding box overlap as intersection area divided by union area, providing the standard metric for evaluating detection localization quality and determining prediction correctness in object detection.

instruction: What is the COCO dataset?
output: COCO (Common Objects in Context) is a large-scale object detection, segmentation, and captioning dataset containing 330,000 images with detailed annotations — 80 object categories with bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and image captions. Think of it like a comprehensive annotated encyclopedia of everyday scenes — not just labels but precise outlines of each object and where they appear. COCO is the standard benchmark for object detection and segmentation, with mean Average Precision (mAP) averaged across IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 as the primary metric. A common misconception is that COCO evaluates only detection — it includes detection (boxes), segmentation (masks), and captioning tracks, each with different evaluation protocols. In one line: COCO is the standard object detection and segmentation benchmark with 80 categories, bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and captions, evaluated using mAP averaged across IoU thresholds.

instruction: What is transfer learning in computer vision?
output: Transfer learning in computer vision fine-tunes models pre-trained on large datasets (typically ImageNet) for new visual tasks — reusing the learned visual representations (edge detectors, texture filters, shape recognizers) rather than learning from scratch. Think of it like a photographer who already knows lighting, composition, and timing — learning wedding photography is much faster than teaching someone who has never held a camera. Pre-trained features transfer because visual patterns are hierarchical and shared across tasks. ImageNet pre-training was the dominant paradigm; CLIP and vision transformers pre-trained on web-scale data have replaced it for many applications. A common misconception is that the entire pre-trained model must be fine-tuned — freezing early layers and training only later layers often works as well with less compute. In one line: vision transfer learning reuses pre-trained visual representations for new tasks, dramatically reducing the data and compute needed by leveraging hierarchical visual features learned from large-scale datasets.

instruction: What is the vision transformer (ViT)?
output: The Vision Transformer applies the transformer architecture directly to images by dividing the image into fixed-size patches, linearly embedding each patch, and processing the sequence of patch embeddings with standard transformer attention. Think of it like treating an image as a sentence where each word is a small image patch — the transformer then attends between patches to build global representations. ViT requires large-scale pre-training to outperform CNNs because it lacks CNNs' translation invariance inductive bias. A common misconception is that ViT replaced CNNs entirely — for small datasets or tasks requiring fine localization, CNNs or hybrid CNN-transformer architectures remain competitive. In one line: ViT applies transformer attention to sequences of image patches, treating images like token sequences — effective at scale with large pre-training datasets but requiring more data than CNNs due to lacking convolutional inductive biases.

instruction: What is CLIP?
output: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) trains a vision encoder and text encoder to produce similar embeddings for matching image-text pairs and dissimilar embeddings for non-matching pairs — learning a joint vision-language embedding space. Think of it like teaching a system to understand that a photo of a dog and the text 'a golden retriever playing fetch' belong together — the training signal comes from millions of image-caption pairs without manual category labels. CLIP enables zero-shot image classification by comparing image embeddings to text embeddings of class descriptions. A common misconception is that CLIP requires class-specific fine-tuning for new categories — it classifies new categories zero-shot by comparing the image to text embeddings of candidate class names. In one line: CLIP learns a joint vision-language embedding space through contrastive training on image-text pairs, enabling zero-shot image classification and cross-modal retrieval without task-specific fine-tuning.

instruction: What is image augmentation and why is it important?
output: Image augmentation applies random transformations to training images — flipping, rotating, cropping, color jittering, cutout, mixup — artificially increasing dataset diversity to reduce overfitting. Think of it like practicing a musical piece in different keys, tempos, and rooms — the underlying skill is the same but practice under varied conditions produces more robust performance. Standard augmentation assumes label-preserving transformations (a flipped cat is still a cat). Advanced methods like mixup (blending two images) and cutmix (replacing patches with another image) create more aggressive regularization. A common misconception is that augmentation is only for small datasets — even ImageNet-scale training benefits significantly from augmentation; AutoAugment and RandAugment learn optimal augmentation policies automatically. In one line: image augmentation applies random label-preserving transformations during training to increase apparent dataset diversity, reducing overfitting and improving model robustness across varied real-world conditions.

instruction: What is the difference between classification accuracy and top-5 accuracy?
output: Top-1 accuracy measures whether the model's single highest-confidence prediction matches the ground truth. Top-5 accuracy measures whether the correct class appears among the model's five highest-confidence predictions. Think of it like two different grading standards — top-1 gives credit only for the exact answer, top-5 gives credit if you listed the right answer anywhere in your top 5 guesses. Top-5 accuracy was the standard ImageNet metric because many visual categories are genuinely ambiguous — 'Labrador Retriever' and 'Golden Retriever' images are difficult to distinguish. A common misconception is that top-5 accuracy is always more informative than top-1 — for applications requiring single correct predictions (medical diagnosis, autonomous driving), top-1 accuracy is the only relevant metric. In one line: top-1 accuracy requires the correct class to be the model's highest-confidence prediction; top-5 accuracy allows it anywhere in the top 5 — top-5 was standard for ImageNet due to genuine inter-class ambiguity.

instruction: What is the difference between batch learning and online learning in terms of data assumptions?
output: Batch learning assumes all training data is available before training begins — the model learns from a fixed static dataset. Online learning assumes data arrives sequentially and the model must update continuously — suitable when data streams arrive over time or the full dataset is too large to store. Think of batch learning like studying all your textbooks before an exam versus online learning like studying each new piece of information as it arrives throughout the semester. Online learning enables adaptation to non-stationary distributions; batch learning enables more stable optimization with multiple passes through data. A common misconception is that mini-batch gradient descent is online learning — mini-batch processes the dataset in small chunks but still operates on a fixed dataset; true online learning processes each example exactly once as it arrives. In one line: batch learning uses a fixed complete dataset; online learning updates continuously from streaming data — batch enables stable multi-pass optimization; online enables adaptation to changing distributions and arbitrarily large data streams.

instruction: What is stream processing vs batch processing in data pipelines?
output: Batch processing collects data over a period, then processes the entire batch together — hourly, daily, or weekly jobs that analyze historical data. Stream processing handles data in real time as it arrives — processing each record or micro-batch with minimal latency. Think of batch processing like end-of-day banking reconciliation versus stream processing like real-time fraud detection that flags transactions the moment they occur. Apache Spark is the dominant batch processing framework; Apache Kafka and Flink are stream processing systems. A common misconception is that stream processing is simply batch processing with smaller batches — streaming requires fundamentally different architecture (state management, windowing, out-of-order handling) that batch systems don't need. In one line: batch processing analyzes historical data in scheduled large jobs; stream processing handles data in real time as it arrives — streaming enables immediate insights but requires more complex stateful architectures.

instruction: What is the difference between OLTP and OLAP databases?
output: OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) databases handle many small read/write transactions — optimized for fast individual record operations, used in operational systems like banking and e-commerce. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) databases handle complex analytical queries over large datasets — optimized for aggregations and scans across millions of records, used for business intelligence. Think of OLTP like a bank teller processing one transaction at a time versus OLAP like an analyst computing average account balances across all customers from last year. Row storage is efficient for OLTP (read/write whole records); columnar storage is efficient for OLAP (scan one column across many rows). A common misconception is that a single database can efficiently serve both — production systems separate OLTP (transactional) and OLAP (analytical) databases for this reason. In one line: OLTP databases optimize for fast individual record operations; OLAP databases optimize for complex aggregations over large datasets — different storage layouts and indexing strategies make them incompatible in practice.

instruction: What is columnar storage and why is it useful for analytics?
output: Columnar storage stores all values of each column together on disk — instead of storing each row contiguously, all values of 'age' are together, all values of 'salary' are together. Think of it like reorganizing a spreadsheet from storing each row on its own page to storing each column on its own page. For analytical queries that aggregate one or few columns across millions of rows (average salary, maximum age), columnar storage reads only the needed columns — dramatically reducing I/O. It also enables excellent compression since column values are homogeneous and often correlated. A common misconception is that columnar storage is always faster — for queries that retrieve all columns of a few rows (OLTP patterns), row storage is faster because columns are scattered. In one line: columnar storage co-locates all values of each column, enabling analytical queries to read only relevant columns and compress homogeneous values efficiently — dramatically reducing I/O for aggregate operations over large datasets.

instruction: What is the difference between supervised and self-supervised learning in computer vision?
output: Supervised computer vision trains on images with human-annotated labels (class, bounding box, segmentation mask). Self-supervised computer vision creates labels automatically from the images themselves — predicting masked patches (MAE), predicting rotation, or contrasting augmented views. Think of supervised learning as classifying photos with a curator who labels each one, versus self-supervised as learning visual structure by predicting what a covered portion of each image looks like — no human labeling required. Self-supervised vision models (DINO, MAE, CLIP) learn powerful general representations from web-scale unlabeled images. A common misconception is that self-supervised vision requires inferior representations — DINO and MAE features outperform supervised ImageNet pre-training on many downstream tasks. In one line: supervised vision requires human-labeled images; self-supervised vision creates labels automatically from image structure — self-supervised representations from web-scale data increasingly match or exceed supervised ImageNet pre-training quality.

instruction: What is masked autoencoder (MAE)?
output: MAE pre-trains vision transformers by masking a large fraction (75%) of image patches and training the model to reconstruct the missing patches from the visible ones — a self-supervised objective that forces the model to learn rich visual representations. Think of it like a jigsaw puzzle where most pieces are hidden and you must reconstruct the full image from just a quarter of the pieces — solving this requires deep understanding of visual structure. MAE's aggressive masking (75% vs BERT's 15%) is possible because images are highly redundant — adjacent patches are similar, making the task tractable but challenging enough to learn useful features. A common misconception is that MAE is just BERT for images — BERT masks language tokens that each carry semantic content; MAE masks image patches that are individually redundant, requiring a much higher masking rate to create a challenging task. In one line: MAE pre-trains ViTs by masking 75% of image patches and reconstructing them, learning rich visual representations through a self-supervised reconstruction objective.

instruction: What is contrastive loss?
output: Contrastive loss trains embeddings by minimizing distance between similar pairs and maximizing distance between dissimilar pairs — the loss is low when similar items are close and dissimilar items are far apart in embedding space. Think of it like a magnetic metaphor — similar items attract each other (minimize distance) while dissimilar items repel (maximize distance). SimCLR uses NT-Xent (Normalized Temperature Cross-Entropy) contrastive loss comparing augmented views. Triplet loss is a variant using anchor-positive-negative triplets. A common misconception is that contrastive loss requires explicit negative mining — modern InfoNCE-based losses treat all other samples in the batch as negatives, simplifying implementation significantly. In one line: contrastive loss trains embeddings by pulling similar pairs together and pushing dissimilar pairs apart, learning representations where geometric proximity reflects semantic similarity.

instruction: What is a knowledge graph?
output: A knowledge graph is a structured representation of facts as entities and typed relationships — (Barack Obama, born_in, Hawaii), (Hawaii, is_part_of, United States) — forming a graph where nodes are entities and edges are relations. Think of it like a giant organized database of real-world facts expressed as triples connecting concepts. Wikidata, Freebase, and Google's Knowledge Graph are prominent examples. Knowledge graphs support reasoning (path-based inference), question answering (SPARQL queries), and grounding AI systems in structured facts. A common misconception is that knowledge graphs capture all world knowledge — they cover explicit stated facts well but struggle with common sense, procedural knowledge, and nuanced relationships. In one line: knowledge graphs represent world knowledge as typed entity-relationship triples, enabling structured fact lookup, reasoning over multi-hop paths, and grounding AI systems in explicit factual knowledge.

instruction: What is graph neural network (GNN)?
output: A graph neural network learns representations of nodes in a graph by iteratively aggregating and transforming features from each node's neighbors — each layer expands the neighborhood considered, building richer representations. Think of it like reputation spreading through a social network — each person's reputation is updated based on their connections, and after several rounds of propagation, each person's representation captures their extended network context. GNNs are applied to molecular property prediction, social network analysis, recommendation systems, and knowledge graph reasoning. A common misconception is that GNNs always outperform MLPs on graph data — for certain graph structures and tasks, simple MLP baselines are competitive, and GNNs struggle with deep aggregation due to oversmoothing. In one line: GNNs learn node representations by iteratively aggregating neighbor features across the graph, enabling learning on relational data where feature interactions follow network structure rather than grid or sequence topology.

instruction: What is the PageRank algorithm?
output: PageRank computes the importance of nodes in a directed graph based on the number and quality of incoming links — a node is important if many important nodes link to it, creating a recursive definition solved through iterative computation. Think of it like academic citations — a paper is important not just if many papers cite it, but if important papers cite it. Google originally used PageRank to rank web pages based on the link graph. The algorithm models a random surfer who follows links uniformly at random (with a damping factor for teleportation back to a random page). A common misconception is that PageRank measures content quality — it measures link-based authority, which correlates with quality but can be manipulated through link farms. In one line: PageRank ranks graph nodes by recursive importance — a node is important if important nodes link to it — solving the circular definition through iterative convergence to a stationary distribution.

instruction: What is word2vec?
output: Word2vec learns word embeddings through two shallow neural network architectures: CBOW (predict a word from its context) and Skip-gram (predict context words from a target word) — both learn to assign similar vectors to words with similar contexts. Think of it like learning word meaning from company — 'bank' and 'shore' appear near 'river', so they end up close in vector space; 'bank' and 'finance' appear near 'money', pulling financial 'bank' toward financial terms. The famous result: vector('king') - vector('man') + vector('woman') ≈ vector('queen') emerges from the geometry. A common misconception is that word2vec captures deep semantic understanding — it captures distributional similarity from co-occurrence patterns, not genuine linguistic understanding, and cannot handle polysemy (different meanings of the same word). In one line: word2vec learns embeddings where similar context implies similar vectors using CBOW or Skip-gram objectives — producing geometric representations where arithmetic captures semantic analogies.

instruction: What is GloVe?
output: GloVe (Global Vectors for Word Representation) learns word embeddings by factorizing the word co-occurrence matrix — training embeddings so their dot products approximate log co-occurrence probabilities across the entire corpus. Think of it like a more principled version of word2vec — instead of implicitly capturing co-occurrence through local context windows, GloVe explicitly models global statistics of the entire corpus. GloVe captures both global corpus statistics and local context information. A common misconception is that GloVe always outperforms word2vec — empirically their performance on downstream tasks is comparable with careful hyperparameter tuning; the main difference is training methodology. In one line: GloVe learns word embeddings by fitting their dot products to global corpus co-occurrence statistics, providing a principled matrix factorization alternative to word2vec's local context window prediction.

instruction: What is ELMo?
output: ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models) produces context-dependent word representations by using a bidirectional LSTM language model — the embedding for each word is a function of the entire input sentence, capturing how word meaning shifts with context. Think of it like understanding 'bank' differently depending on whether the sentence is about rivers or finance — ELMo gives 'bank' a different vector in each context. ELMo was a breakthrough before transformers, demonstrating that pre-trained contextual representations transfer powerfully to downstream tasks. A common misconception is that ELMo and BERT are equivalent — ELMo uses shallow bidirectional LSTMs (separate forward and backward); BERT uses deep transformers with full bidirectional attention, producing richer representations. In one line: ELMo produces context-dependent word representations from bidirectional LSTM language models, enabling the same word to have different embeddings in different contexts — a key step toward BERT and contextual pre-training.

instruction: What is the difference between a language model and a conversational AI?
output: A language model predicts the next token given previous tokens — it has no inherent goal of being helpful, understanding user intent, or maintaining conversation flow. Conversational AI is built on top of language models with fine-tuning, prompt engineering, and alignment to understand user goals, maintain dialogue context, and respond helpfully and appropriately. Think of a language model as a very sophisticated autocomplete versus conversational AI as an assistant who understands what you need. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are conversational AI products built on top of language models through instruction tuning and RLHF. A common misconception is that all language models are conversational — base models respond to prompts by text continuation, not intentional conversation; alignment training is what creates conversational behavior. In one line: language models predict next tokens; conversational AI adds intent understanding, dialogue management, and helpful response generation through fine-tuning and alignment — the same underlying model behaves very differently with and without alignment training.

instruction: What is speech recognition and how does it work?
output: Speech recognition (ASR) converts spoken audio to text — processing acoustic features (spectrograms or raw waveforms), modeling phonemes or subword units, and decoding the most likely word sequence. Think of it like reading lips using ears — converting continuous audio waveforms into discrete text tokens. Modern end-to-end models (Whisper, wav2vec 2.0) learn to map audio directly to text without explicit phoneme modeling. CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) loss handles the alignment between audio frames and text tokens without requiring frame-level supervision. A common misconception is that speech recognition solves natural language understanding — ASR produces text transcripts; understanding the meaning of that text requires separate NLU processing. In one line: speech recognition converts audio to text by learning acoustic-to-text mappings end-to-end — modern models like Whisper achieve near-human accuracy on English using transformer architectures trained on web-scale audio-transcript pairs.

instruction: What is text-to-speech (TTS)?
output: Text-to-speech synthesizes spoken audio from text — converting written words into natural-sounding speech with appropriate prosody, rhythm, and voice quality. Think of it like the reverse of speech recognition — transforming discrete text tokens back into continuous audio waveforms that sound natural. Modern neural TTS (Tacotron, FastSpeech, VITS) generates spectrogram representations that are converted to audio by neural vocoders. Voice cloning extends TTS to reproduce a specific person's voice from a few seconds of reference audio. A common misconception is that TTS is only useful for accessibility — TTS is the foundation of voice assistants, audiobook generation, real-time dubbing, and conversational AI audio output. In one line: TTS synthesizes natural speech from text using neural models that generate spectrograms and vocoders that convert them to audio — enabling voice assistants, audiobooks, and speech interfaces with human-like prosody.

instruction: What is the difference between discriminative and generative models for image generation?
output: Discriminative models for images (classifiers, detectors) learn to map images to labels — they cannot generate new images. Generative models learn the image distribution and can synthesize new images — GANs generate through adversarial training, VAEs through variational inference, and diffusion models through iterative denoising. Think of it like the difference between a movie critic (discriminative) and a film director (generative) — the critic judges films; the director creates them. Modern image generation (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney) uses diffusion models conditioned on text prompts. A common misconception is that image generation models understand the world — they learn statistical patterns of pixel arrangements, which can produce realistic images without any physical or semantic understanding. In one line: discriminative image models classify what they see; generative image models synthesize new images — modern text-to-image systems use diffusion models conditioned on text embeddings to generate photorealistic images from descriptions.

instruction: What is the difference between conditional and unconditional generation?
output: Unconditional generation samples from the full learned data distribution without any guidance — the model generates freely from any point in the distribution. Conditional generation samples from the distribution given some conditioning signal — text prompt, class label, style image, or partial data. Think of unconditional generation like asking an artist to 'draw anything' versus conditional generation like asking them to 'draw a sunset over the ocean in impressionist style.' Conditioning significantly constrains the output space and enables user control. Text-to-image, text-to-speech, and instruction-following are all forms of conditional generation. A common misconception is that unconditional generation is easier — learning the full unconditional distribution is actually harder because the model must model all modes without guidance. In one line: unconditional generation samples freely from the full data distribution; conditional generation constrains sampling to match a provided conditioning signal — enabling user-controlled, task-specific generation.

instruction: What is the difference between a pixel-level and feature-level loss in image generation?
output: Pixel-level loss (L1, L2) measures differences between generated and target images at every pixel — penalizing any deviation in pixel intensity. Feature-level (perceptual) loss measures differences in high-level representations from a pre-trained network (like VGG) — penalizing semantic content differences rather than pixel-exact matches. Think of pixel loss like asking 'are the exact same pixels set?' and perceptual loss like asking 'does this image look like the same thing?' Pixel loss tends to produce blurry outputs because averaging over multiple plausible pixel configurations minimizes MSE. Perceptual loss produces sharper, more realistic results at the cost of pixel-accuracy. A common misconception is that pixel-level accuracy implies visual quality — a slightly shifted image has high pixel error but looks nearly identical to humans. In one line: pixel-level loss penalizes exact pixel differences producing blurry outputs; perceptual loss penalizes semantic feature differences producing sharper, more visually realistic results.

instruction: What is the FID score for evaluating generative models?
output: FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) measures the distance between the distribution of real images and generated images — computing the Fréchet distance between multivariate Gaussians fit to Inception features of real and generated image sets. Think of it like comparing the statistical 'personality' of two art collections — FID measures how similarly distributed generated images are to real ones in feature space. Lower FID means more realistic and diverse generated images. FID became the standard image generation metric because it correlates better with human perceptual quality than pixel-based metrics. A common misconception is that low FID always means high quality — FID measures distribution similarity, not individual image quality; a model can memorize training images to get perfect FID with no generalization. In one line: FID measures generation quality by comparing Inception feature distributions of real and generated images — lower FID indicates more realistic and diverse generation, correlating well with human perceptual judgment.

instruction: What is mode collapse in GANs?
output: Mode collapse occurs when a GAN generator learns to produce only a few types of outputs that fool the discriminator — ignoring the full diversity of the real data distribution. Think of it like a counterfeiter who perfects one fake bill denomination and ignores all others because the inspector only catches certain types — the generator finds a small set of 'safe' outputs and repeats them. Severe mode collapse produces visually similar or identical images regardless of the input noise. Solutions include Wasserstein GAN (better gradient signal), minibatch discrimination (penalizing similar outputs in a batch), and diverse training signals. A common misconception is that mode collapse means the generator produces poor quality images — mode-collapsed outputs can be individually realistic but lack diversity. In one line: mode collapse occurs when the GAN generator produces limited variety of outputs by finding a small set of samples that reliably fool the discriminator — producing realistic but non-diverse outputs.

instruction: What is the Wasserstein distance and why is it used in GANs?
output: Wasserstein distance (Earth Mover's Distance) measures the minimum cost of transforming one probability distribution into another — providing meaningful gradients even when distributions have non-overlapping support. Think of it like measuring how much earth you'd need to move to reshape one pile of sand into another shape — it gives a continuous, meaningful distance even when the shapes don't overlap at all. Standard GAN training with Jensen-Shannon divergence produces zero or undefined gradients when distributions don't overlap (early in training), causing instability. Wasserstein GAN uses the Wasserstein distance as loss, enabling smoother training with better gradient signal. A common misconception is that WGAN completely solved GAN training instability — WGAN-GP (with gradient penalty) significantly improved stability but GAN training remains challenging. In one line: Wasserstein distance provides meaningful gradients even when real and generated distributions don't overlap, solving the vanishing/exploding gradient problem in standard GAN training at the cost of more complex optimization.

instruction: What is the difference between image translation and image generation?
output: Image generation creates images from scratch — from noise (diffusion, GAN), from text (text-to-image), or from random latent vectors. Image translation converts images from one domain to another — photo to painting, summer to winter, black-and-white to color, sketch to photo. Think of generation as creating something from nothing versus translation as transforming something that exists. pix2pix and CycleGAN are canonical image translation models. CycleGAN enables unpaired translation (no one-to-one correspondence needed between domain examples). A common misconception is that image translation is simply applying a filter — it learns the full statistical translation between domains, capturing structural transformations that go far beyond fixed filters. In one line: image generation creates images from scratch; image translation converts images between domains — CycleGAN enables unpaired translation without matching examples, while pix2pix requires paired domain examples.

instruction: What is style transfer in computer vision?
output: Neural style transfer creates an image that combines the content of one image (its objects and structure) with the style of another (its textures, colors, and artistic patterns) — by optimizing an image to match the content features of the content image and the Gram matrix statistics of the style image. Think of it like painting a photograph of Paris in the style of Van Gogh — the Eiffel Tower stays recognizable (content) but rendered in swirling colorful brushstrokes (style). Gatys et al. (2015) introduced style transfer using pre-trained VGG features. Fast neural style transfer learns a feedforward network to apply a specific style in real time. A common misconception is that style transfer requires retraining a model — Gatys-style transfer optimizes the output image with fixed pre-trained features; only fast style transfer trains a new network. In one line: neural style transfer creates images combining one image's content with another's artistic style by optimizing intermediate VGG layer representations — content from later layers, style from Gram matrices of earlier layers.

instruction: What is super-resolution in computer vision?
output: Super-resolution reconstructs a high-resolution image from a low-resolution input — recovering fine details that were not present in the input by learning realistic high-frequency patterns from training data. Think of it like restoring an old blurry photograph — the missing detail must be hallucinated from learned priors about what realistic images look like. SRCNN, SRGAN, and Real-ESRGAN are prominent architectures. GAN-based super-resolution can hallucinate perceptually realistic textures that aren't in the original — useful for visual quality but potentially misleading in forensic contexts. A common misconception is that super-resolution recovers true lost detail — it generates plausible detail from learned patterns, which is visually convincing but not necessarily physically accurate. In one line: super-resolution reconstructs high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs by learning to generate plausible high-frequency details — producing visually sharp results that hallucinate realistic texture rather than recovering truly lost information.

instruction: What is the difference between supervised contrastive learning and self-supervised contrastive learning?
output: Self-supervised contrastive learning uses augmented views of the same image as positive pairs and other images in the batch as negatives — no labels required. Supervised contrastive learning uses label information to define positives — all images of the same class are positive pairs, all images of different classes are negatives. Think of self-supervised as 'the same photo from different angles should be similar' versus supervised as 'all photos of dogs should be similar, regardless of angle or breed.' Supervised contrastive learning produces better class-discriminative representations because class membership is the explicit positive signal. A common misconception is that supervised contrastive loss is the same as cross-entropy — it produces more structured embedding spaces with better inter-class separation and intra-class cohesion than cross-entropy. In one line: self-supervised contrastive learning uses augmented views as positives; supervised contrastive learning uses class labels — supervised version produces better class-discriminative representations by making all same-class examples attract each other.

instruction: What is the difference between precision, recall, and F1 in object detection?
output: In object detection, a true positive is a predicted box with IoU > threshold against a ground truth box and correct class. Precision is the fraction of predicted boxes that are true positives. Recall is the fraction of ground truth objects that have at least one matching predicted box. F1 (harmonic mean of precision and recall) balances both. Mean Average Precision (mAP) extends this by computing the area under the precision-recall curve for each class and averaging — capturing performance across all confidence thresholds. A common misconception is that high precision in detection means few missed objects — high precision only means most predictions are correct; a system could predict very few boxes and have perfect precision but terrible recall. In one line: detection precision measures box prediction correctness; recall measures ground truth coverage; mAP summarizes the precision-recall tradeoff across all thresholds and classes — the standard comprehensive detection evaluation metric.

instruction: What is the difference between a variational and a standard autoencoder?
output: A standard autoencoder encodes each input to a fixed point in latent space — the latent code has no statistical constraint and nearby points may decode to gibberish. A variational autoencoder encodes each input to a distribution (mean and variance) in latent space — the KL divergence term in the loss forces the latent space to be smooth and continuous. Think of standard autoencoders as placing books on any shelf in a library with no organization, versus VAEs enforcing a systematic catalog — VAEs ensure the latent space is navigable and generates meaningful samples. A common misconception is that VAEs and GANs are equivalent generative models — VAEs have an explicit likelihood objective producing blurrier but more stable outputs; GANs use adversarial training producing sharper but less stable outputs. In one line: standard autoencoders map inputs to fixed latent points without structure; VAEs map inputs to distributions, enforcing smooth continuous latent spaces through KL regularization — enabling meaningful interpolation and generation.

instruction: What is the reparameterization trick in VAEs?
output: The reparameterization trick makes sampling from the VAE's latent distribution differentiable — instead of sampling z directly from N(μ, σ²) (non-differentiable), it samples ε from N(0,1) and computes z = μ + σ·ε — moving the randomness outside the computation graph so gradients flow through μ and σ. Think of it like the difference between randomly choosing which path to take in a maze (non-differentiable) versus choosing a random direction offset and computing the resulting path deterministically — the second allows gradient flow through the path computation. Without the reparameterization trick, the VAE's reconstruction loss cannot be backpropagated through the sampling operation. A common misconception is that the reparameterization trick is specific to VAEs — it applies to any model that requires differentiable sampling from a distribution. In one line: the reparameterization trick makes VAE sampling differentiable by expressing z = μ + σ·ε with fixed ε ~ N(0,1), moving randomness outside the computation graph so gradients flow through the distribution parameters.

instruction: What is the difference between a discriminator and a classifier?
output: A classifier assigns inputs to one of a predefined set of semantic categories — cat, dog, car. A GAN discriminator assigns inputs a score representing how real versus fake they appear — it outputs a probability that its input came from the real data distribution rather than the generator. Think of a classifier like a taxonomist who categorizes species versus a discriminator like a quality control inspector who determines whether a product is genuine or counterfeit. The discriminator's training signal guides the generator to produce more realistic outputs; it's not classifying semantic content but reality. A common misconception is that the discriminator classifies which class the image belongs to — it only classifies real vs. fake; semantic understanding is not required. In one line: classifiers assign semantic category labels; GAN discriminators assess authenticity (real vs. generated) — discriminators guide generators toward realistic outputs without needing any semantic understanding of image content.

instruction: What is the difference between deterministic and stochastic models?
output: Deterministic models produce the same output for the same input every time — given identical conditions, the result is identical. Stochastic models incorporate randomness — the same input can produce different outputs due to sampling from probability distributions. Think of deterministic as a calculator (same computation, same result always) versus stochastic as rolling dice under constraints (same rules, different outcomes each time). Neural network inference is typically deterministic (with fixed weights and no dropout); training is stochastic (random batch sampling, random weight initialization). Generative models are stochastic at inference — sampling different noise produces different outputs. A common misconception is that stochastic models are less reliable — for generation tasks, stochasticity is a feature that produces diverse, creative outputs rather than a bug. In one line: deterministic models produce identical outputs for identical inputs; stochastic models incorporate randomness — generation models are intentionally stochastic to produce diverse outputs from the same conditioning.

instruction: What is the difference between supervised representation learning and self-supervised representation learning?
output: Supervised representation learning trains encoders using human-labeled data as the training signal — the representations are optimized to separate labeled classes. Self-supervised representation learning trains encoders using automatically generated signals from the data itself — predicting masked content, predicting augmented views, or predicting next tokens. Think of it like learning to organize a filing system by being told what everything is (supervised) versus discovering natural groupings by observing which documents tend to appear together (self-supervised). Self-supervised representations are more general because they aren't optimized for specific label sets; supervised representations are better discriminators for their specific label space. A common misconception is that self-supervised always produces inferior representations — DINO, MAE, and GPT-style self-supervised representations outperform supervised ImageNet features on many downstream tasks. In one line: supervised representation learning optimizes for label discrimination; self-supervised learning creates representations from data structure alone — self-supervised representations are more general and increasingly match supervised quality without human annotation.

instruction: What is embedding dimensionality and how do you choose it?
output: Embedding dimensionality is the length of the vector used to represent each entity (token, word, item) — higher dimensions enable more expressive representations but require more parameters and computation. Think of it like the number of axes used to describe a city — 2D gives latitude/longitude (limited), 10D captures neighborhood, altitude, demographics, economy (richer). Common choices: 64-256 for smaller models, 768-1024 for BERT-scale, 4096-12288 for large LLMs. The optimal dimension depends on vocabulary size, task complexity, and compute budget. A common misconception is that larger embeddings are always better — diminishing returns appear quickly; going from 256 to 512 dimensions helps much more than from 4096 to 8192 for the same task. In one line: embedding dimensionality controls representational capacity — larger embeddings capture more nuanced relationships but with diminishing returns, and optimal size depends on vocabulary size, model scale, and computational constraints.

instruction: What is the difference between hard negative mining and random negative sampling?
output: Random negative sampling selects negatives uniformly at random — easy, computationally cheap, but many negatives are trivially distinguishable from positives. Hard negative mining selects the most challenging negatives — examples that are almost positive, near the decision boundary — providing the most informative training signal. Think of practice exams — easy questions don't improve performance much; hard questions at your skill boundary advance learning fastest. Hard negatives accelerate metric learning but risk false negatives (true positives incorrectly labeled negative) degrading training. A common misconception is that harder negatives always produce better models — too-hard negatives (actual positives mislabeled) hurt training; semi-hard negatives (closer than a margin but not false negatives) are often optimal. In one line: hard negative mining selects challenging near-positive examples for contrastive training, providing more informative gradient signal than random sampling but requiring careful curation to avoid false negatives.

instruction: What is the difference between transposed convolution and bilinear upsampling?
output: Bilinear upsampling uses fixed mathematical interpolation to increase spatial resolution — the output values are weighted averages of nearby input values following fixed rules. Transposed convolution learns upsampling weights from data — the same interpolation but with learned rather than fixed weights, optimized for the specific task. Think of bilinear as a fixed zoom lens and transposed convolution as a smart zoom that learns to fill in details appropriate for the content. Bilinear upsampling is cheaper and more stable; transposed convolution is more expressive but prone to checkerboard artifacts (from uneven overlap in the convolution). Checkerboard artifacts can be avoided by using bilinear upsampling followed by a regular convolution instead of transposed convolution. A common misconception is that transposed convolution always produces better results — bilinear + convolution often produces cleaner outputs with fewer artifacts. In one line: bilinear upsampling uses fixed interpolation; transposed convolution learns upsampling weights — learned upsampling is more expressive but prone to checkerboard artifacts that bilinear upsampling avoids.

instruction: What is the difference between a shallow network and a wide network?
output: A shallow network has few layers but may have many neurons per layer — depth is low, width is high. A deep network has many layers but may be narrower at each layer. Width adds parameters at each layer increasing representational capacity within that computation step. Depth adds compositionality — each layer transforms representations for the next, enabling hierarchical feature learning. Think of width like a wide single-floor building (many parallel computations at one level) versus depth like a tall skyscraper (many successive transformations). The universal approximation theorem guarantees that sufficiently wide shallow networks can approximate any function — but in practice depth is far more efficient for many tasks. A common misconception is that wide and deep networks are equivalent in practice — depth provides exponential expressiveness advantages for hierarchical tasks like vision and language. In one line: width adds parallel computational capacity within layers; depth adds successive transformations enabling hierarchical feature learning — depth is empirically more efficient for complex pattern recognition than equivalent width.

instruction: What is self-attention vs cross-attention vs masked attention?
output: Self-attention allows each position to attend to all other positions in the same sequence — queries, keys, and values all come from the same input. Cross-attention allows positions in one sequence to attend to positions in another — queries from one sequence, keys and values from another. Masked (causal) attention restricts each position to attending only to previous positions in the same sequence — using a triangular mask. Think of self-attention as a team where everyone can consult everyone, cross-attention as translators consulting source documents, and masked attention as readers who can only reference what they've already read. Transformers use all three: encoder uses self-attention, decoder uses masked self-attention + cross-attention. A common misconception is that masked attention is a separate mechanism from self-attention — it is self-attention with a masking pattern applied to the attention weights. In one line: self-attention attends within one sequence; cross-attention attends from one sequence to another; masked attention restricts self-attention to previous positions — transformers use all three in encoder-decoder and decoder-only architectures.

instruction: What is the difference between a shallow and deep copy in computation graphs?
output: In the context of deep learning computation graphs, a shallow copy shares the underlying tensor data — modifying one modifies the other. A detached copy (`.detach()`) creates a new tensor with the same data but disconnected from the computation graph — no gradients flow through it. Think of it like sharing versus printing a document — sharing means changes affect the original; printing creates an independent copy. Detaching tensors is critical in RL and GANs where you want to update one network without gradients flowing into another. A common misconception is that `.detach()` copies the data — it creates a new tensor node but shares the underlying storage; use `.clone().detach()` for a true independent copy. In one line: detaching a tensor disconnects it from the computation graph so no gradients flow through it — essential in adversarial and RL training where separate networks must be updated independently.

instruction: What is automatic differentiation?
output: Automatic differentiation (autograd) computes exact gradients of any differentiable computation by recording operations in a computation graph and applying the chain rule — neither numerical approximation (finite differences) nor symbolic algebra. Think of it like tracking every arithmetic step in a derivation and applying the chain rule systematically — exact, efficient, and requiring no human calculus. PyTorch's autograd builds a dynamic computation graph during the forward pass and traverses it backwards during `.backward()`. A common misconception is that autograd and backpropagation are the same thing — backpropagation is a specific algorithm for neural networks; autograd is the general framework for automatic differentiation of any computation. In one line: automatic differentiation computes exact gradients by recording operations in a computation graph and applying the chain rule — enabling gradient-based optimization of arbitrary differentiable programs without manual derivative calculations.

instruction: What is the difference between eager and graph execution in deep learning frameworks?
output: Eager execution runs operations immediately as they are called — the computation happens right away and results are available instantly, making debugging intuitive. Graph execution traces the computation, builds an optimized graph, and then executes the entire graph — enabling compiler optimizations but making debugging harder. Think of eager as executing code line by line like an interpreter, while graph mode compiles the whole program first for efficient execution. PyTorch defaults to eager execution; TensorFlow originally used graph execution (now supports both). `torch.compile` and `tf.function` bridge the gap — tracing eager code to produce optimized graphs. A common misconception is that eager execution is always slower — for research and debugging, eager mode is preferred; for production deployment, compiled graphs can be significantly faster. In one line: eager execution runs operations immediately for easy debugging; graph execution traces and optimizes the full computation for speed — most modern frameworks support both through JIT compilation.

instruction: What is ONNX?
output: ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) is an open format for representing machine learning models — enabling models trained in one framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow) to be exported and run in different runtime environments (TensorRT, OpenVINO, ONNX Runtime). Think of it like a universal file format for neural network models — just as PDF ensures a document looks the same in any viewer, ONNX ensures a model runs consistently in any ONNX-compatible runtime. ONNX enables deployment optimization without retraining — exporting to ONNX and running in optimized runtimes can accelerate inference significantly. A common misconception is that ONNX supports every model perfectly — dynamic shapes, custom operations, and complex control flow can cause export failures or silent numerical differences. In one line: ONNX provides a standard model exchange format enabling models trained in one framework to run in any compatible runtime — facilitating deployment optimization and cross-framework compatibility.

instruction: What is model serving and why is it different from training?
output: Model serving is deploying a trained model to production to make predictions on real user requests — optimizing for latency, throughput, and cost rather than accuracy during training. Serving has different requirements than training: no gradient computation, often batch size 1 or small, strict latency requirements, high availability needs, and horizontal scaling. Think of training as developing a recipe in a test kitchen versus serving as running a restaurant — completely different operations, infrastructure, and success metrics. Model serving solutions include TorchServe, Triton Inference Server, and vLLM (for LLMs). A common misconception is that training and serving use the same infrastructure — training is compute-intensive batch processing; serving is latency-sensitive online inference requiring different hardware, scaling, and monitoring. In one line: model serving deploys trained models for real-time predictions optimizing for latency, throughput, and cost — fundamentally different from training's batch compute requirements and needing specialized infrastructure.

instruction: What is tensor parallelism?
output: Tensor parallelism splits individual layer computations across multiple devices — for example, splitting a large matrix multiplication by distributing different rows or columns to different GPUs and synchronizing partial results. Think of it like splitting a large jigsaw puzzle between multiple people who each work on their section and then combine to see the full picture. Megatron-LM popularized tensor parallelism for training LLMs with layers too large for single GPU memory. Combined with pipeline and data parallelism in 3D parallelism for training 100B+ parameter models. A common misconception is that tensor parallelism is the same as model parallelism — tensor parallelism splits individual operations; model parallelism (pipeline parallelism) splits the model by assigning entire layers to different devices. In one line: tensor parallelism distributes individual layer computations across multiple devices, enabling single layers too large for one GPU's memory to be computed collaboratively.

instruction: What is pipeline parallelism?
output: Pipeline parallelism partitions model layers into stages assigned to different devices — device 1 runs layers 1-8, device 2 runs layers 9-16, and so on — with micro-batches pipelining through the stages to reduce idle time. Think of it like a factory assembly line where different workstations handle different stages — car body on station 1, engine installation on station 2, painting on station 3 — with multiple cars moving through simultaneously so no station sits idle. GPipe and PipeDream are foundational pipeline parallelism systems. A common misconception is that pipeline parallelism eliminates bubble overhead — pipeline startup and teardown create idle time ('bubble') that reduces efficiency; larger pipelines have proportionally larger bubbles. In one line: pipeline parallelism assigns model layers to different devices with micro-batches pipelining through stages, enabling models too deep for single devices but introducing bubble overhead from pipeline startup and teardown.

instruction: What is the difference between model inference speed and throughput?
output: Inference speed (latency) is how quickly a single request gets a response — measured in milliseconds per request. Throughput is how many requests can be processed per unit time — measured in requests per second or tokens per second. Think of it like a restaurant — latency is how long one diner waits for their meal; throughput is how many meals the restaurant serves per hour. They often trade off: batching many requests together improves throughput (more parallel GPU utilization) but increases individual request latency. LLM serving optimizes both through continuous batching (adding new requests to running batches without waiting). A common misconception is that faster inference always means higher throughput — a very fast single-request model that processes one request at a time may have lower throughput than a slightly slower model that batches 100 requests simultaneously. In one line: latency measures single-request response time; throughput measures requests processed per unit time — batching improves throughput at the cost of latency, with continuous batching enabling LLM systems to optimize both simultaneously.
